chunk_summary: Refeeding safety in severe acute malnutrition (SAM) with CKD or CLD requires careful electrolyte monitoring, individualized supplementation, and cautious nutritional escalation to prevent refeeding syndrome and fluid overload.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks of refeeding syndrome in children with SAM and CKD or CLD.
Learn appropriate potassium, phosphate, and magnesium supplementation strategies in CKD stage 4–5.
Recognize the importance of monitoring electrolytes and fluid balance during nutritional escalation.
Identify safe feeding practices in cholestasis or cirrhosis with ascites.
key_facts: Children with SAM and CKD/CLD are at high risk for hypoglycemia, hypothermia, infection, and fluid overload.
Indian stabilization protocols recommend potassium (3–4 mEq/kg/day) and magnesium supplementation with 'no added salt' diets for uncomplicated SAM.
In CKD stage 4–5, potassium and phosphate supplementation should be based on measured values and dialysis losses.
Electrolytes should be monitored closely during the first 72 hours of refeeding, often daily or more frequently in unstable cases.
In cholestasis/cirrhosis with ascites, energy-dense feeds and early enteral support are preferred to meet nutritional targets within fluid constraints.
reference_quote: Indian stabilization protocols commonly include potassium (about 3–4 mEq/kg/day) and magnesium supplementation with 'no added salt' diets during initial stabilization, reflecting typical total-body potassium and magnesium deficits in uncomplicated SAM.
document_key_topics: Refeeding syndrome in SAM with CKD/CLD
